metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the bin-to-canonical-ID mapping (or the prerequisite blocks themselves) is threaded to the Pass-2 verifier path so `prerequisite_attribution_valid` can be answered `yes`/`no` from evidence, not only inferred from the finding text against the subject plan.
evidence: 'Pass 2 sets `yes` only when the indexed prerequisite finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin, `no` for wrong or unprovable attribution'
Existing verify path (verify_finding_listing + verification_model) receives subject plan + findings list only — no prerequisite block context is injected
ABSENCE: no specification of how bin-to-canonical-ID mapping reaches the Pass-2 verifier
findings.finding: Pass-2 cannot verify `prerequisite_attribution_valid` without the bin's prerequisite context: the plan specifies Pass 2 sets `prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes` only when the indexed finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin, but the Pass-2 verifier receives only the subject plan + the findings list — not the prerequisite blocks themselves. Without knowing which canonical IDs were in which bin, the verifier has no basis to confirm or refute attribution. The plan does not specify how the bin-to-ID mapping is threaded to the Pass-2 path for prerequisite findings.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend findings with optional `prerequisite_id`' / Pass-2 verification of `prerequisite_attribution_valid`
scenarios: A prerequisite finding carrying the wrong canonical ID passes Pass-2 with `prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes` because the verifier sees only the finding text and the subject plan, and cannot cross-check against which prerequisite was actually in the bin.
Pass 2 consistently marks all prerequisite findings `na` (the veto default) because there is no context to answer `yes` or `no`, making the attribution veto mechanism a no-op.
findings.suggested_fix: Include the prerequisite canonical ID(s) for the bin in the Pass-2 finding listing entry (e.g. as a `prerequisite_id` annotation on the finding passed to `verify_finding_listing`), so the verifier can check attribution against the stated bin membership without receiving the full prerequisite plan text (which would violate the isolation AC).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `ItemRecord` extension question: either explicitly allow adding `gold_prerequisite_id`/`pred_prerequisite_id` fields to `ItemRecord` (updating the 'leave unchanged' constraint), or name a prerequisite-specific record type and explain how `prerequisite_fidelity_report` reuses `parity_report`'s typed interface.
evidence: Current parity.py ItemRecord has fields: valid, decision, errored, label, cost, latency_s, gold_criterion, pred_criterion.
Plan says leave ItemRecord unchanged, yet also requires scoring pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id.
ABSENCE: ItemRecord has no prerequisite_id fields. A function that computes pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id must read these values from somewhere.
The plan says 'prerequisite records as pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id' — implying ItemRecord or a subtype carries these fields.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `prerequisite_fidelity_report` in `parity.py` that scores `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id`, but simultaneously states that `ItemRecord`, `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, `attribution_accuracy`, and `container_fidelity_report` must remain unchanged. The existing `ItemRecord` carries `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` fields for G3/G4 attribution. It is unverified whether the new prerequisite attribution scoring can be implemented within the existing `ItemRecord` shape without adding analogous `gold_prerequisite_id`/`pred_prerequisite_id` fields — which would require changing `ItemRecord` contrary to the plan's own constraint.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add a dedicated `prerequisite_fidelity_report` in `parity.py`; leave `ItemRecord`, `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, `attribution_accuracy`, and `container_fidelity_report` unchanged. The new report reuses general recall and false-acceptance calculations but scores attribution with prerequisite records as `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id` over authoritative canonical IDs.'
scenarios: If ItemRecord is left truly unchanged, the prerequisite fidelity report must either (a) use `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` to carry prerequisite IDs (conflating two semantically different attribution axes), or (b) define a parallel record type, in which case `prerequisite_fidelity_report` cannot reuse `parity_report` directly since `parity_report` is typed to `Sequence[ItemRecord]`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `gold_prerequisite_id: str | None = None` and `pred_prerequisite_id: str | None = None` to `ItemRecord` (a backward-compatible additive change mirroring the existing `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` addition), and relax the 'leave ItemRecord unchanged' constraint to 'leave ItemRecord's existing fields unchanged'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what prerequisite context (canonical IDs, bin membership per finding index) is injected into the verifier's instructions or input schema to enable Pass 2 to set `prerequisite_attribution_valid` correctly, and confirm this injection does not leak prerequisite material into the non-prerequisite finder prompts.
evidence: Plan: 'Pass 2 sets `yes` only when the indexed prerequisite finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin, `no` for wrong or unprovable attribution, and `na` for non-prerequisite findings.'
passes.py PASS_VERIFIER = 'plan-review-verifier' — the verifier prompt is a library file, not modified by this plan.
ABSENCE: No mention of modifying the verifier system prompt, its input schema, or adding a prerequisite-bin-to-finding index alongside the findings list passed to verify.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that Pass 2 sets `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to `yes` only when the indexed prerequisite finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin, `no` for wrong or unprovable attribution, and `na` for non-prerequisite findings — but the verifier prompt and its input shape are never updated to provide the prerequisite context (canonical IDs, source bin membership) that would make this judgment possible. The verifier currently receives only the plan text and findings.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Pass 2 sets `yes` only when the indexed prerequisite finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin, `no` for wrong or unprovable attribution, and `na` for non-prerequisite findings.'
scenarios: Pass 2 verifier has no way to know which findings are prerequisite findings or what their canonical IDs are, so it answers `na` for all of them, making `prerequisite_attribution_valid` always `na` and the attribution veto inoperative.
Alternatively, implementors hard-wire prerequisite ID injection into the verifier instructions, contaminating the non-prerequisite prompt isolation the plan elsewhere mandates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the record type (either a new `PrerequisiteItemRecord` dataclass or an extension of `ItemRecord`) that carries `pred_prerequisite_id` and `gold_prerequisite_id` for use by `prerequisite_fidelity_report`, and clarify how it relates to the leave-`ItemRecord`-unchanged constraint.
evidence: Plan: 'scores attribution with prerequisite records as `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id` over authoritative canonical IDs'
Plan: 'leave `ItemRecord`, `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, `attribution_accuracy`, and `container_fidelity_report` unchanged'
parity.py ItemRecord has only `gold_criterion: str | None` and `pred_criterion: str | None` for attribution — no prerequisite_id fields.
ABSENCE: no `PrerequisiteItemRecord` or analogous type defined to carry prerequisite-specific gold/pred IDs for the fidelity report.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a `prerequisite_fidelity_report` in `parity.py` that 'scores attribution with prerequisite records as `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id` over authoritative canonical IDs', but simultaneously mandates that `ItemRecord`, `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, `attribution_accuracy`, and `container_fidelity_report` are left unchanged. `ItemRecord` only has `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` for attribution scoring; no new field carrier for prerequisite IDs is defined, making the described scoring formula unimplementable without either extending `ItemRecord` (violating the leave-unchanged constraint) or specifying a different record type entirely.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add a dedicated `prerequisite_fidelity_report` in `parity.py`; leave `ItemRecord`, `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, `attribution_accuracy`, and `container_fidelity_report` unchanged.'
scenarios: Implementor repurposes `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` to carry prerequisite IDs, silently breaking `attribution_accuracy()` and `container_fidelity_report()` if they receive these records.
Implementor adds fields to `ItemRecord`, directly contradicting the leave-unchanged constraint and potentially breaking existing container fidelity tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a separate `PrerequisiteItemRecord` dataclass alongside `ItemRecord` in `parity.py` carrying `gold_prerequisite_id: str | None` and `pred_prerequisite_id: str | None`, then implement `prerequisite_fidelity_report` against that type — satisfying both the attribution scoring requirement and the unchanged-ItemRecord constraint.

findings.finding: The plan extends `fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval` and `parity.container_fidelity_report` with eight gold prerequisite sets, but `container_fidelity_report` and `ItemRecord` are purpose-built for G3/G4 per-child attribution: `ItemRecord.gold_criterion` and `pred_criterion` are documented as `'G3' | 'G4' | None`, and `attribution_accuracy()` computes `pred_criterion == gold_criterion` — a comparison designed for criterion IDs, not canonical prerequisite IDs. Repurposing these fields with prerequisite canonical IDs (e.g. `'acaf-bb4d-...'`) would produce an always-failing or always-passing attribution score depending on whether any gold value matches a prerequisite ID string, making the fidelity gate silently incorrect for the prerequisite use-case.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval and parity.container_fidelity_report with at least eight gold prerequisite sets'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `prerequisite_fidelity_report(baseline, candidate, ...)` function that reuses `parity_report` for recall/false-accept but defines attribution accuracy as `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id` — avoiding repurposing the G3/G4-specific `gold_criterion` field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'leave `ItemRecord` unchanged' and scoring `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id`: either name a new `PrerequisiteItemRecord` (or add optional `gold_prerequisite_id`/`pred_prerequisite_id` fields to `ItemRecord` with explicit compatibility notes), or specify how existing `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` slots are repurposed.
evidence: Plan: 'scores attribution with prerequisite records as `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id` over authoritative canonical IDs'
Plan: 'leave `ItemRecord`, `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, `attribution_accuracy`, and `container_fidelity_report` unchanged'
parity.py:40-54: `ItemRecord` has fields `gold_criterion: str | None` and `pred_criterion: str | None` — no prerequisite-ID field exists
Adding prerequisite-ID scoring requires either extending `ItemRecord` (contradicting the plan's 'unchanged' constraint) or defining a new record type (not mentioned)
findings.finding: `prerequisite_fidelity_report` in `parity.py` is specified to score `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id` over authoritative canonical IDs, but the plan simultaneously states `ItemRecord` is left unchanged — and `ItemRecord` carries only `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` fields, with no prerequisite-ID fields. The report cannot score prerequisite-ID attribution without either a new field on `ItemRecord` or a parallel record type, neither of which the plan names.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add a dedicated `prerequisite_fidelity_report` in `parity.py`; leave `ItemRecord`, `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, `attribution_accuracy`, and `container_fidelity_report` unchanged.'
scenarios: Implementor adds `pred_prerequisite_id`/`gold_prerequisite_id` to `ItemRecord` and breaks the 'unchanged' constraint, silently changing the container fidelity report's field assumptions.
Implementor introduces a parallel record type but `prerequisite_fidelity_report` cannot reuse the shared `parity_report()` recall/false-accept logic without wrapping or duplicating it.
Implementor reuses `pred_criterion`/`gold_criterion` as surrogate prerequisite-ID fields, creating semantic confusion between G3/G4 criterion attribution and prerequisite canonical-ID attribution.
findings.suggested_fix: Add optional `gold_prerequisite_id: str | None = None` and `pred_prerequisite_id: str | None = None` fields to `ItemRecord` (frozen dataclass allows this additively since container scoring only reads `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion`), and explicitly note these fields are `na`-equivalent (both None) for non-prerequisite records, preserving `container_fidelity_report` byte-identity.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that parity.container_fidelity_report can be extended with eight gold prerequisite sets for attribution scoring, but the existing function's attribution logic hard-codes CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4') and scores only records whose gold_criterion is in that two-value fixed vocabulary. Extending it for arbitrary prerequisite canonical IDs (which are ticket ID strings, not a closed two-value enum) requires non-trivial changes to ItemRecord, attribution_accuracy(), and container_fidelity_report() that the plan does not specify.
findings.location: Implementation section, last bullet: 'Extend fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval and parity.container_fidelity_report with at least eight gold prerequisite sets'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a prerequisite-specific variant (e.g., prerequisite_fidelity_report) that scores attribution against canonical prerequisite IDs rather than the G3/G4 enum, or parameterize attribution_accuracy() with the expected-criterion vocabulary and document the CONTAINER_CRITERIA constant as unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `tests/interfaces/llm/` path: either explicitly add this directory to the plan's file impact and create it, or refile the two named integration test files under an existing `tests/interfaces/` subdirectory (e.g. `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/` for the workflow test and `tests/interfaces/contracts/` for the isolation test) and update the Verification commands accordingly.
evidence: Directory listing of tests/interfaces/ shows: adapters.py, contracts/, facades/, io/, lifecycle/, queries/, store/ — no llm/ subdirectory
Verification section references tests/interfaces/llm/test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py and tests/interfaces/llm/test_plan_review_workflow.py
The plan does not enumerate tests/interfaces/llm/ as a new directory to create
findings.finding: The Verification section names test files under `tests/interfaces/llm/` (specifically `tests/interfaces/llm/test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py` and `tests/interfaces/llm/test_plan_review_workflow.py`), but no `tests/interfaces/llm/` directory exists in the repo — the analogous directories are `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/`, `tests/interfaces/contracts/`, etc.
findings.location: Verification section: 'Run .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/interfaces/llm/test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py tests/interfaces/llm/test_plan_review_workflow.py'
scenarios: Running the specified pytest command fails immediately with 'no such file or directory' since the parent directory does not exist
CI breaks on the verification step without the author understanding the real path convention
findings.suggested_fix: Move the isolation test to `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py` and the workflow test to `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_workflow.py`, consistent with how existing plan-review integration tests are organized (e.g. `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what context the Pass-2 verifier prompt receives to answer `prerequisite_attribution_valid` — confirm whether the verifier is given the canonical IDs expected for each finding's `prerequisite_id`, or clarify why the finding's self-reported `prerequisite_id` alone is sufficient for a reliable yes/no answer.
evidence: Plan states: 'Pass 2 sets yes only when the indexed prerequisite finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin, no for wrong or unprovable attribution, and na for non-prerequisite findings.'

Current verify_models.py Binary fields are sub-questions about a finding's grounding (is_verifiable, evidence_entails_finding, etc.) — not relational questions about which external artifact sourced the finding.

The plan does not specify whether the verifier prompt receives the per-bin prerequisite canonical IDs alongside the findings, which is needed for the verifier to compare the finding's `prerequisite_id` against the bin's expected ID.
ABSENCE: no existing Binary field crosses a finding against an external-artifact identifier; this is a new category of verification sub-question.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that Pass 2 can set `prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes/no` based on whether 'the indexed prerequisite finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin', but does not verify how the verifier knows which source bin a finding originated from. The Pass-2 verifier currently receives a flat list of findings; the plan extends findings with optional `prerequisite_id` (the finder's output field) but does not specify whether the verifier receives the bin-to-finding mapping or whether `prerequisite_id` in the finding is sufficient for the verifier to perform the attribution check without additional context injection.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend findings with optional `prerequisite_id`. Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to the shared `Binary` model… Pass 2 sets `yes` only when the indexed prerequisite finding names the exact canonical ID in its source bin'
scenarios: If the verifier prompt does not receive the bin-to-canonical-ID mapping, it cannot reliably answer yes/no for attribution_valid — it can only check whether the finding's `prerequisite_id` field is non-empty and plausible.
A finding with a plausible-but-wrong `prerequisite_id` (attribution-confusion gold set) would receive `yes` from a verifier that cannot cross-check against the bin's authoritative ID.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) include the expected canonical ID alongside each finding in the verifier's listing (analogous to how the completion sub-call pre-states `_container_child` for structural findings), or (b) make Pass 2 a per-bin sub-call that receives only the findings for that bin's canonical ID so attribution is unambiguous by construction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `tests/unit/test_plan_review_passes.py` and `tests/unit/test_plan_review_sizing.py` to the file impact as new files with their purpose described, or correct the Verification command to reference the actual files that cover the `passes.py` and `sizing.py` changes introduced by this story.

Plan file impact does not enumerate either file as a new creation
test_plan_review_prerequisites.py is also new (absent) but at least it is the primary new unit test for this story's logic

scenarios: pytest command fails with 'file not found' for both files

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `plan_review_decide_input` schema extension (or equivalent input key) that carries `prerequisite_coverage` records into the decide step, including the field name, type, and whether it is required or optional.
evidence: Plan: '`plan_review_decide` validates record completeness before partitioning and adds affected records to the indeterminate partition'
workflow_ops.py plan_review_decide uses input_schema='plan_review_decide_input' and reads only findings, verifications, det_blocking, det_advisory from ctx.inputs.
ABSENCE: no specification of adding `prerequisite_coverage` to `plan_review_decide_input` schema or its input-key name.
findings.finding: The plan says '`plan_review_decide` validates record completeness before partitioning and adds affected records to the indeterminate partition' but `plan_review_decide`'s current input schema (`plan_review_decide_input`) does not include `prerequisite_coverage`. No extension of this input schema is specified, so there is no defined place for `prerequisite_coverage` data to enter the decide step.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Thread `prerequisite_coverage` through both workflow branches into `plan_review_decide` and `plan_review_coach`.'
scenarios: Implementor adds `prerequisite_coverage` to `plan_review_decide_input` without a schema definition, breaking the closed-schema contract.
Implementor routes the prerequisite record completeness check outside the decide step (e.g. in a new precheck step), diverging from the plan's stated threading.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the shared `Binary` model change with AC3: either (a) scope `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to a prerequisite-only subtype/mixin that is never instantiated on the parent/child path, so the Binary schema is genuinely unchanged for those paths, or (b) reword AC3 to 'functionally unchanged (no behavioral difference)' and accept the schema addition.
evidence: Implementation: 'Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to the shared `Binary` model in `review_kernel/verify_models.py`; it defaults to `na`, joins `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, and is excluded from `GRADED_BINARY`'
AC3: 'Existing parent and child review behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged when no direct prerequisite route applies.'
These two statements are in direct tension: the shared model change affects all callers of the Binary model, not only the prerequisite route.
findings.finding: The Implementation section modifies the shared `Binary` model in `review_kernel/verify_models.py` by adding `prerequisite_attribution_valid` (defaults to `na`), yet AC3 requires that 'Existing parent and child review behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged when no direct prerequisite route applies.' Adding a field to a shared serialized/compared model — even one that defaults to `na` — changes the wire/storage shape of every parent and child review result, making AC3 unmeetable as literally written.
findings.location: Implementation section (paragraph on `prerequisite_attribution_valid` in `verify_models.py`) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: A consumer that diffs or byte-compares serialized Pass-2 outputs for parent/child reviews across a deploy boundary will see a new field appear, violating AC3.
A regression test that snapshot-tests the Binary schema will break even when no prerequisite route fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a `PrerequisiteVerifyRecord` subclass of `Binary` that adds `prerequisite_attribution_valid`, used only in the prerequisite verification path. The base `Binary` model used by parent/child paths remains schema-identical, satisfying AC3 literally.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `prerequisite_attribution_valid` belongs in the shared `Binary` model in `review_kernel/verify_models.py` or only in `plan_review_verification_model`; if the former, specify the code-review contract impact and sidecar compatibility obligations.
evidence: Plan: 'Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to the shared `Binary` model in `review_kernel/verify_models.py`; it defaults to `na`, joins `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, and is excluded from `GRADED_BINARY`'
verify_models.py `_build_binary()` is consumed by both `verification_model()` (code-review) and `plan_review_verification_model()` — adding a field there affects both.
ABSENCE: plan never specifies whether this field should be in the shared base Binary or only in the plan-review extension model.
findings.finding: The plan adds `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to the shared `Binary` model in `review_kernel/verify_models.py` (the same model code-review and every other gate uses), but never specifies whether it should live in the shared model or only in `plan_review_verification_model`. The shared `_build_binary()` loop iterates `GRADED_BINARY` plus hardcoded veto fields; adding a prerequisite-context-dependent veto to the shared model forces code-review verifications to emit this field with no semantic meaning in that context, contradicting the per-gate isolation principle demonstrated by `plan_review_verification_model` vs `verification_model`.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to the shared `Binary` model in `review_kernel/verify_models.py`'
scenarios: Code-review verifications start emitting `prerequisite_attribution_valid` fields with no semantic content, polluting sidecar outputs and potentially confusing Pass-3 behavior if future changes misread it.
The shared `verification` contract registered via `register_verification_contract()` changes shape for all review gates, violating the expansion posture for code-review consumers.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `prerequisite_attribution_valid` into `plan_review_verification_model`'s `_build_binary`-equivalent extension (or a plan-review-specific override) so it has no effect on code-review or the shared `verification` contract, consistent with how `plan_review_verification_model` already extends the base with plan-specific severity axes.

findings.finding: The plan proposes extending `fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval` and `parity.container_fidelity_report` to evaluate prerequisite sets, but both functions are purpose-built for the G3/G4 child-container domain: `packing_spot_eval` works over `children: list[dict]` keyed by `ticket_id` and `_container_child`, and `container_fidelity_report` computes per-child-ticket-ID recall/attribution. The prerequisite domain uses different units (prerequisite plan texts, not child ticket dicts) and a different attribution key (`prerequisite_id`, not `_container_child`). Forcing this harness onto prerequisite evaluation is a golden-hammer: it muddies both harnesses and likely requires enough internal adaptation to justify a dedicated prerequisite fidelity helper.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval and parity.container_fidelity_report with at least eight gold prerequisite sets'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism for the changes to `plan_review_decide`, `plan_review_coach`, and `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` — for example, an expand-contract deployment note, a feature flag that makes `prerequisite_coverage` an opt-in parameter with a nil default that short-circuits the new logic, or a documented revert commit boundary.
evidence: 'Thread `prerequisite_coverage` through both workflow branches into `plan_review_decide` and `plan_review_coach`.'
'In `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`, check that flag before all PASS or BLOCK branches and force top-level `INDETERMINATE`.'
No rollback, feature-flag, or expand-contract language appears anywhere in the plan.
The plan's Upstream contract and Acceptance Criteria sections are likewise silent on back-out.
findings.finding: The plan modifies three existing interfaces — `plan_review_decide`, `plan_review_coach`, and `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` — as well as the `Binary` model schema, but specifies no rollback path, back-out procedure, or expand-contract sequence for any of these changes. T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism, not merely acknowledgement that the changes are conditional.
findings.location: Implementation section — paragraphs on 'Thread `prerequisite_coverage` through both workflow branches' and 'In `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`, check that flag before all PASS or BLOCK branches'
scenarios: A bug in prerequisite coverage threading causes `plan_review_decide` to raise on all calls (not just prerequisite ones); no back-out path is documented.
A serialization regression in `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` forces all reviews INDETERMINATE; the team has no documented procedure to disable only the prerequisite check.
findings.suggested_fix: Make `prerequisite_coverage` an optional parameter defaulting to `None` on `plan_review_decide` and `plan_review_coach`; in `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`, gate the new check on `coverage is not None`. Document that setting the parameter to `None` (the default for all existing callers) is the back-out posture. This is both the expand-contract pattern and the rollback path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting the prerequisite-review architectural decisions: the veto-semantics `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field and its exclusion from `GRADED_BINARY`, the two-route Pass-1 isolation contract, and the rationale for forcing INDETERMINATE on any indeterminate prerequisite record.
evidence: 'Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to the shared `Binary` model in `review_kernel/verify_models.py`; it defaults to `na`, joins `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, and is excluded from `GRADED_BINARY`, so it is a veto rather than a validity contributor.'
'Thread `prerequisite_coverage` through both workflow branches into `plan_review_decide` and `plan_review_coach`.'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions across component boundaries — a veto-semantics `prerequisite_attribution_valid` binary field excluded from grading, a new two-route Pass-1 batch runner, an isolation contract enforced at test time, and a new `prerequisite_coverage` pipeline stage threaded through both workflow branches — yet no ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Implementation section (all sub-bullets) and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A future maintainer adding a new criterion type discovers undocumented veto-vs-grade semantics and accidentally adds a field to GRADED_BINARY, breaking the veto invariant.
A new engineer reads the orchestrator and cannot understand why `prerequisite_indeterminate=true` forces INDETERMINATE even when other findings would BLOCK — no rationale is recorded.
The two-route Pass-1 architecture becomes a source of confusion when the next story extends the batch runner without knowing the isolation invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a short ADR (or an `## Architecture` subsection in AGENTS.md) covering: (1) why `prerequisite_attribution_valid` is a veto rather than a graded binary and what exclusion from `GRADED_BINARY` means for future field additions; (2) the isolation invariant (non-prerequisite prompts must not contain prerequisite IDs/text) and how it is enforced; (3) the rationale for unsigned-INDETERMINATE escalation on any indeterminate prerequisite record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability instrumentation (at minimum a structured log entry and a counter metric) for each error disposition path (evaluation-error, output-invalid, attribution-invalid) in the prerequisite batch runner so operators can detect and triage elevated indeterminate rates.
evidence: No mention of logging, metrics, tracing, or alerting anywhere in the Implementation or Acceptance Criteria sections for the prerequisite batch runner
The error paths (evaluation-error, output-invalid, attribution-invalid) are defined but there is no stated requirement to instrument them as observable signals
findings.finding: The new LLM prerequisite-coverage call path has no described observability instrumentation (metrics, structured logs, traces, or alerts). Operators cannot detect elevated error rates, latency spikes, or systematic indeterminate outcomes on this path.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with a prerequisite-only route' and 'Non-context provider failure emits indeterminate/evaluation-error'
scenarios: A systematic LLM provider failure silently produces a flood of INDETERMINATE verdicts that operators only discover when downstream consumers notice missing certifications
A prompt-sizing regression causes chronic input-too-large indeterminate records that go undetected until a parity run
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add: 'Emit a structured log entry at WARNING level and increment a counter metric (e.g. plan_review_prerequisite_coverage_error_total labeled by reason_code) whenever a prerequisite record is assigned an indeterminate disposition. Attach a trace span to each prerequisite batch LLM call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum per-call timeout for prerequisite LLM calls and define the disposition emitted (indeterminate/evaluation-error) when that timeout is exceeded, consistent with the existing error-handling model.
evidence: The plan specifies handling for context-limit errors and non-context provider failures but does not mention timeouts or a maximum wait duration
ANTI-FP check: this is an external LLM call — an explicit slow-provider scenario (not just hard failure) is in scope for T5b dependency-failure blast radius
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for the LLM calls made by the prerequisite batch runner. A slow (not failing) provider causes the call to hang indefinitely, blocking the entire review workflow — including unrelated work that shares the same process or thread pool.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with a prerequisite-only route'; Acceptance Criteria — no timeout AC present
scenarios: LLM provider experiences elevated latency; prerequisite review calls hang for minutes, stalling the review orchestrator and causing cascading timeouts in any caller waiting on plan_review_decide output
A large batch of prerequisites causes a per-call timeout to be reached; without a defined behavior, the outcome is undefined
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'Each prerequisite coverage LLM call is subject to a configurable timeout (default: X seconds). On timeout, treat the affected bin identically to a non-context provider failure: emit indeterminate/evaluation-error for every unresolved ID in that bin.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the scope and retirement path of the PlanRelationSnapshot preload (e.g., 'the preload is created once per review invocation, held only for that invocation's duration, and not cached across calls') to prevent cross-request contamination and unbounded accumulation.
evidence: Plan states 'exactly one relation preload supplies both pin creation and prerequisite routing' but gives no lifetime, scope, or cleanup specification
The preload contains pinned material and plan text — data that is per-ticket and per-review-run, so cross-request sharing would be incorrect
No RETIRE or cleanup path is described anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The lifecycle scope of the shared `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload is unspecified — the plan does not state whether it is request-scoped, session-scoped, or potentially cached/singleton. Without a stated scope and retirement path, implementers may make it module-level, introducing cross-request staleness, cross-tenant contamination, or unbounded accumulation.
findings.location: Implementation — 'One shared relation preload supplies both material-pin creation and prerequisite routing'; Upstream contract section
scenarios: A module-level preload cache is introduced for efficiency; stale prerequisite plan text from a previous review run is used for a new review, producing incorrect findings
Under concurrent review calls, a shared mutable preload reference is overwritten mid-flight, producing findings that attribute text from the wrong ticket
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Upstream contract or Implementation section: 'The PlanRelationSnapshot is request-scoped: it is constructed at the start of each plan_review invocation and released (goes out of scope) when that invocation completes. It is never stored in a module-level or process-level cache.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete motivation section: describe what currently goes wrong (or is undetectable) when a plan is inconsistent with a direct prerequisite — include an Expected-vs-Actual example, a named gap in the current review output, or a concrete user/system problem caused by the absence of this check.
evidence: Plan states only: 'Evaluate whether a ticket plan remains consistent with each direct prerequisite' — a description of what will be built, not why it is needed.
No section describes what currently happens when a plan is inconsistent with a prerequisite, nor any evidence that the existing review misses such cases.
No failing test, no repro steps, no incident, no user report motivating the addition.
findings.finding: The plan adds an entirely new prerequisite-consistency review mechanism but never establishes that the current behavior is wrong or insufficient — there is no reproduction of a missed inconsistency, no Expected-vs-Actual, no named defect or gap, and no stated user/system problem the current review causes by lacking this capability.
findings.location: Goal / Upstream contract / Implementation sections
scenarios: The mechanism is built and the capability was already achievable through existing review paths, delivering no net value while adding complexity and maintenance burden.
If the upstream certified interfaces change, the untested motivation makes it harder to judge whether the feature remains necessary.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Problem' or 'Motivation' section, e.g.: 'Currently, Pass-1 reviews each plan in isolation; if a plan contradicts a prerequisite's stated approach (e.g. uses a deprecated API the prerequisite explicitly replaced), the reviewer has no signal. Example: [concrete case]. This causes [concrete harm — e.g. plans that PASS review but are blocked at integration].  This story adds a dedicated prerequisite-consistency criterion to catch such gaps.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `PlanRelationSnapshot` (and its fields `related_material`, `relation_kind`, `readable_state`, `pinned_material`) is actually importable from the codebase before implementation begins, or add an explicit 'blocked until `acaf` / `75de` are merged' precondition to the plan.
evidence: search_files('class PlanRelationSnapshot', path='src') → zero matches
Ticket 75de-02b0-d454-410b status=open; signed manifest lists 'dep absent tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py', 'dep absent tests/unit/test_plan_material_pin_config.py' etc.
Ticket acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (the pin story defining PlanRelationSnapshot) referenced in cache reviews as 'no implementation of PlanRelationSnapshot exists in the codebase'
Plan Implementation: 'It receives the subject plan plus stable-order whole prerequisite blocks from the single preload' — impossible without the type existing
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The pin interface supplies one review-time `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload keyed by authoritative canonical ticket ID, including relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and the ticket plan text needed by review' as an already-available upstream contract to consume, but `PlanRelationSnapshot` does not exist anywhere in `src/`. A full search of `src/` returns zero matches. Both upstream tickets (`75de-02b0-d454-410b` and `acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c`) are certified PASS but remain `status: open` with most implementation files listed as `dep absent` in their signed manifests, meaning the type has been designed and certified but not yet merged into the codebase. Every mechanism in this plan — the single shared preload, prerequisite routing, the `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval` corpus, and the isolation AC — depends on this unimplemented interface.
findings.location: Upstream contract section: 'This story implements against the certified material-pin interface from `75de-02b0-d454-410b`'
scenarios: An implementer attempts to write the prerequisite-only route in the batch runner and finds no `PlanRelationSnapshot` type to import — the entire routing conditional has nothing to key on.

The isolation integration test (`test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py`) cannot assert 'exactly one relation preload supplies both pin creation and prerequisite routing' because no preload mechanism exists yet.
The `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` function needs to consume `related_material` from `PlanRelationSnapshot` — field names and types are undefined in the current repo.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit prerequisite gate: 'This story requires `acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c` to be merged (not merely certified) before implementation begins. Verify with `from rebar.llm.plan_review.relation_snapshot import PlanRelationSnapshot` returning no ImportError.' Add this as a Verification step or a blocking precondition AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the ordering rule for 'stable-order prerequisite blocks' (e.g. deterministic sort by canonical ID, or preload insertion order if the preload itself guarantees stable ordering), so bin packing and the tail-conflict gold set are reproducible.
evidence: 'packs as many blocks as fit, and invokes the dedicated `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage` structured-output contract'
'two tail-conflict where the conflict is last in stable bin order'
ABSENCE: no definition of what 'stable order' means for the prerequisite sequence
findings.finding: The prerequisite block ordering rule ('stable order') is never defined, making both the packing behavior and the `tail-conflict` gold sets non-reproducible: the plan specifies batching packs 'stable-order whole prerequisite blocks' and that the `tail-conflict` evaluation set requires the conflict to be 'last in stable bin order' — but no ordering rule is given (insertion order from the preload? alphabetical by canonical ID? `depends_on` before inverse `blocks`?).
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with a prerequisite-only route' and 'Add new opt-in `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval` coverage'
scenarios: Different runs produce different bin packings because the preload snapshot returns relations in nondeterministic order, violating the 'stable bins' guarantee.
The tail-conflict gold set cannot be authored reliably because 'last in stable bin order' is ambiguous — a prerequisite at the end under alphabetical order may be first under insertion order.
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'stable order is the canonical ID sort order of the readable pinned prerequisites in the preload, applied once at routing time and held constant for the run.' This matches how existing stable identifiers (criterion IDs, bin IDs) are handled elsewhere.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Implementation section explaining why the parallel prerequisite route in the batch runner was chosen (e.g. reuses the existing sizing/escalation ladder, produces Pass-2-verifiable findings in the standard schema, achieves prompt-level isolation without a separate gate step).
evidence: ABSENCE: no statement of why the parallel batch-runner route is preferred over a post-Pass-1 injection step or a standalone prerequisite gate step
The 'Upstream contract' section explains provenance dependencies but not approach selection rationale

The plan specifies what to build and how, but not why this architecture fits the codebase (reuse of existing ladder/escalation machinery, consistent Pass-2/3 finding schema, isolation without a separate gate)
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for the chosen approach — a parallel prerequisite-only route in the production batch runner with a dedicated contract — over simpler alternatives such as a post-Pass-1 enrichment step with direct partition injection, leaving reviewers and implementers without the design-intent grounding required to navigate trade-offs during implementation.
findings.location: Implementation section (overall approach) and Upstream contract section
scenarios: Implementer encounters an edge case (e.g. whether prerequisite findings should participate in the advisory cap) and has no design-intent guidance to decide correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why the prerequisite context-limit path uses recursive bin-halving rather than adapting the existing `pass1_with_ladder` / `pack_container_bins` infrastructure, or explicitly reuse `is_context_limit_error` and `models_at_or_above` as shared primitives (which the plan already implies by naming `models_at_or_above()`) and note the deliberate algorithmic difference.
evidence: sizing.py already contains: `pass1_with_ladder` (batch → single → escalate → failure finding), `is_context_limit_error`, `models_at_or_above`, `pack_container_bins` (greedy bin-packing with oversized tracking).
The plan proposes recursive halving (different algorithm from greedy bin-pack) but uses the same `models_at_or_above()` escalation function.

The prerequisite version differs in that 'bins contain whole prerequisite blocks' (analogous to 'content never chunked' in pass1_with_ladder) and maps to `indeterminate/evaluation-error` rather than a failure finding — these differences may justify a distinct path, but the plan does not articulate them.
ABSENCE: no justification for why recursive halving is chosen over the existing greedy pack approach.
findings.finding: The plan's recursive bin-halving on context-limit errors ('split a failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves recursively, escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`, exhaustion at the largest window emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error`') re-derives the size-handling ladder already implemented in `sizing.pass1_with_ladder` (batch → one-criterion-per-call → model escalation → too-big failure). The plan does not explain why this prerequisite-specific re-derivation is justified over adapting the existing `pass1_with_ladder` / `is_context_limit_error` / `models_at_or_above` infrastructure.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Size the actual prerequisite prompt topology… On a context-limit error, split a failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves recursively. Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Exhaustion at the largest window emits its record as `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=input-too-large`.'
scenarios: If the recursive halving and `pass1_with_ladder` both exist without shared infrastructure, context-limit detection logic (the `is_context_limit_error` heuristic strings) must be kept in sync across two code paths.
Future changes to the model ladder require updates in both paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a named prompt ID constant for the prerequisite coverage call (e.g. `PASS_PREREQUISITE = 'plan-review-prerequisite'`) in `passes.py` and specify a corresponding prompt library file, analogous to `PASS_COMPLETION` and its `plan-review-completion` prompt.
evidence: passes.py defines PASS_FINDER, PASS_VERIFIER, PASS_NOVELTY, PASS_COMPLETION, PASS_COACH, PASS_ISF, PASS_CONTAINER, PASS_CONTRADICTION, PASS_COMMENT_TRAIL as module-level prompt ID constants.
ABSENCE: no `PASS_PREREQUISITE` or equivalent prompt ID constant is defined in the plan for the prerequisite coverage call.

The integration test asserts 'non-prerequisite finder and verifier system prompt, user prompt, and instructions contain neither a prerequisite canonical ID nor prerequisite plan text' — this test needs a prompt ID to know which requests to classify as prerequisite vs non-prerequisite.
findings.finding: The prerequisite LLM call invokes a 'dedicated `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage` structured-output contract' but no prompt ID constant is defined for the prerequisite reviewer prompt (analogous to `PASS_FINDER = 'plan-review-finder'`, `PASS_COMPLETION = 'plan-review-completion'`, etc. in passes.py). Every other plan-review pass prompt is named as a module-level constant; the prerequisite call has no named prompt, making it undiscoverable by the prompt library, the caching system, and the isolation test.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with a prerequisite-only route. It receives the subject plan plus stable-order whole prerequisite blocks from the single preload, packs as many blocks as fit, and invokes the dedicated `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage` structured-output contract.'
scenarios: Implementor uses an ad-hoc inline prompt string (breaking the '_resolve_system' prompt-library discipline) or registers an unlisted prompt ID that the cache-split and isolation test cannot identify.
The isolation test cannot distinguish prerequisite requests from non-prerequisite requests without a named reviewer or prompt constant.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Any indeterminate prerequisite coverage record makes the entire review unsigned INDETERMINATE and prevents plan-review certification,' but never names which workflow step or code site implements this escalation. The existing pipeline (plan_review_precheck → plan_review_assemble_criteria → batch finder → verify → plan_review_decide → plan_review_coach) has no step that reads prerequisite coverage records and gates the terminal verdict or the attest.py certification path on them. Without an assigned implementation site, an executor cannot wire this escalation.
findings.location: Implementation section (bullet 6): 'Any indeterminate prerequisite coverage record makes the entire review unsigned INDETERMINATE and prevents certification.' Also AC: 'Any indeterminate prerequisite coverage record makes the whole review unsigned INDETERMINATE and prevents plan-review certification.'

findings.finding: The plan extends the Pass-2 binary schema with `optional prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes|no|na` but never specifies WHERE this field attaches in the existing `plan_review_verification` model (to `Binary`, to `PlanSeverityAttrs`, or as a new top-level wrapper field), nor whether it is graded (counts toward validity) or a veto (like `cited_reference_accurate`), nor whether it carries a na-default (like `respects_artifact_altitude`) or an insufficient-default. These distinctions are load-bearing: the existing `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` frozenset, `GRADED_BINARY` set, and `_build_binary()` builder in `verify_models.py` each must be updated correctly, and the wrong placement changes the validity math for every finding verified by this criterion.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the existing finding schema with optional prerequisite_id and the index-keyed Pass 2 binary schema with optional prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes|no|na. Pass 2 sets yes only for the indexed finding's exact in-bin canonical ID.'
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to `Binary` with na-default (join `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`) and exclude from `GRADED_BINARY` (making it a veto-style field, not a validity contributor); Pass 2 sets `yes` only for the exact in-bin canonical ID, `na` for all other findings.'

findings.finding: The plan says to 'Extend `fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval` and `parity.container_fidelity_report` with at least eight gold prerequisite sets,' but both functions are architecturally purpose-built for the G3/G4 child-container domain. `packing_spot_eval` constructs `ItemRecord` with `gold_criterion ∈ {G3, G4}` and `pred_criterion` from container findings; `container_fidelity_report` scores `attribution_accuracy` against `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')`. The plan never specifies whether 'extend' means new overloads, new parallel functions, or in-place modification, and never maps the prerequisite-ID attribution dimension to the `gold_criterion`/`pred_criterion` vocabulary — leaving the executor with no protocol for building prerequisite ItemRecords or scoring attribution accuracy across prerequisite sets.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the established opt-in live evaluation proves packed fidelity meets the one-per-call contract' and Acceptance Criteria — 'The established opt-in live evaluation proves packed fidelity meets the one-per-call contract.'

findings.finding: The integration test AC requires proving 'pin collection plus criterion routing share one preload without prompt leakage,' but 'prompt leakage' is not defined anywhere in the plan — not as a schema check, not as a prompt-content assertion, not as an absence of prerequisite material in non-focused criterion inputs. An executor cannot write a deterministic, passing/failing test for an undefined property.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add an integration test proving pin collection plus criterion routing share one preload without prompt leakage.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'without prompt leakage' with a measurable predicate, e.g.: 'the general-criterion finder's assembled prompt inputs (system + user message) contain none of the prerequisite plan texts or canonical IDs that appear in the focused-criterion input.'

findings.finding: The Verification section names test suites by label ('Run focused registry, Pass-1/Pass-2, sizing, and fidelity spot-eval tests') without specifying executable commands or paths, leaving an executor unable to run verification without prior project knowledge.
findings.location: Verification section, first bullet

findings.finding: The new prerequisite-consistency criterion requires an LLM to emit a `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` output, but the plan never names a Pydantic model factory or a `register_contract()` call for this output schema. Every LLM call in the existing system is backed by a registered contract (e.g., `plan_review_findings`, `plan_review_verification`, `plan_review_completion`), and the registration site is the machine-checkable invariant that proves the contract exists. The plan specifies the record's field names and semantics but never specifies the structured-output contract name, the factory function, or its registration site.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Require exactly one PrerequisiteCoverageRecord per readable pinned canonical ID: {prerequisite_id: …, disposition: …, findings: …, reason_code?: …, detail?: …}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section naming the factory (e.g., `_prerequisite_coverage_model()`) and its registration: `contracts.register_contract('plan_review_prerequisite_coverage', _prerequisite_coverage_model)` in `passes.py` or a new `prerequisite.py`, mirroring the `_pass2_completion_model` pattern.

findings.finding: The AC 'The established opt-in live evaluation proves packed fidelity meets the one-per-call contract' asserts that a prerequisite-specific opt-in eval harness already exists, but no such harness is present: the existing fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval covers only G3/G4 container criteria, and no prerequisite gold sets (the eight required by the Implementation) exist anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: AC: 'The established opt-in live evaluation proves packed fidelity meets the one-per-call contract.' Also Implementation: 'Extend fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval and parity.container_fidelity_report with at least eight gold prerequisite sets...'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'The established opt-in live evaluation' to 'A new opt-in live evaluation (prerequisite_packing_spot_eval, to be added to fidelity_spot_eval.py)' and add the new eval spec file path to the Implementation's explicit deliverables list.

findings.finding: 'Prompt leakage' in the required integration test is undefined. The plan mandates 'Add an integration test proving pin collection plus criterion routing share one preload without prompt leakage,' but neither the plan nor the codebase defines what 'prompt leakage' means in the criterion-routing context. Without a definition, an executor cannot write a deterministic, falsifiable test for this property.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Add an integration test proving pin collection plus criterion routing share one preload without prompt leakage.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'without prompt leakage' with a concrete assertion: e.g., 'assert that for every non-prerequisite-criterion RunRequest emitted during the review, neither `system_prompt` nor `instructions` contains text from any prerequisite ticket's plan body.'

findings.finding: 'Tail-conflict' is used as a gold-corpus category without definition; an executor cannot construct the two required tail-conflict gold sets without knowing what distinguishes them from single-conflict sets.
findings.location: Implementation, final paragraph: 'two tail-conflict'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical: 'two tail-conflict (conflict prerequisite is last in stable bin order, verifying no late-truncation suppression)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `test_plan_review_passes.py` (or a named companion test) uses captured-request snapshot comparison for non-prerequisite paths to prove byte-for-byte prompt identity, not merely behavioral equivalence.
evidence: AC asserts 'byte-for-byte unchanged'
No mention of snapshot tests, golden-output fixtures, or request-capture assertions for the non-prerequisite path in the Implementation section
findings.finding: The AC 'Existing parent and child review behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged when no direct prerequisite route applies' is a very strong claim, but the plan maps it to `test_plan_review_passes.py` without specifying that the test uses snapshot/golden-output comparison. A behavioral equivalence test (same findings, same schema) would pass this file without proving byte-for-byte identity of prompts and outputs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Existing parent and child review behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged when no direct prerequisite route applies'; Verification — `pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_passes.py`

findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'For non-prerequisite paths, capture emitted requests and assert byte-for-byte equality against pre-change snapshots stored in the test fixtures directory.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Implementation or Verification section a description of how the largest-window exhaustion path is triggered in unit tests (e.g., a configurable stub that raises a context-limit error for every model in `models_at_or_above()`, or a fixture that sets a very small max-window for testing).
evidence: Implementation says 'Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Exhaustion at the largest window emits its record as `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=input-too-large`'
No test fixture or injection strategy described for simulating exhaustion of all models in the escalation ladder
findings.finding: The AC 'Largest-window singleton exhaustion emits one `indeterminate/evaluation-error` record with `detail=input-too-large` before top-level remediation' maps to `test_plan_review_prerequisites.py`, but the plan does not describe a test fixture, injection point, or mock mechanism for reliably triggering the context-limit-exhaustion code path in the test suite.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Largest-window singleton exhaustion emits one `indeterminate/evaluation-error` record with `detail=input-too-large`'; Verification — `pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly what content constitutes one 'prerequisite block' for batching (e.g., plan text only, or plan text + relation kind + state), so the batch runner and sizing adapter use the same definition.
evidence: 'stable-order whole prerequisite blocks from the single preload' — the composition of a 'block' is never defined
Upstream contract section names 'relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and the ticket plan text' as preload fields, but the plan does not state which subset forms one block
findings.finding: The content of a 'prerequisite block' for packing purposes is not explicitly defined — the plan says 'whole prerequisite blocks from the single preload' but never states whether a block consists of plan text only, plan text plus relation metadata (kind, state), or the full `PlanRelationSnapshot` payload. An implementer must infer this from the upstream interface spec rather than the plan itself.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` to the file impact list with reason 'Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` na-default Binary sub-question and include it in `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`.'
